mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Ghana on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, on the west coast. Ghana faces the Atlantic Ocean, with Togo, Burkina Faso and Côte d'Ivoire (Ivory Coast) as its neighbours 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Ghana?
mapQuestions.answer: Accra, on the Atlantic coast. About 2.5 million people live in the city itself, and many more in the wider metro area.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Ghana speak?
mapQuestions.answer: English is the official language, but Ghanaians grow up speaking Twi, Ga, Ewe, Dagbani, Fante and 80+ other languages. Twi (the language of the Akan people) is the most widely spoken.

mapQuestions.question: Why is Ghana an important date in African history?
mapQuestions.answer: Ghana was the FIRST country in sub-Saharan Africa to gain independence from European colonial rule, in 1957, led by Kwame Nkrumah. Many other African countries followed in the years after.

mapQuestions.question: Which crop is Ghana world-famous for?
mapQuestions.answer: Cocoa. Ghana is one of the world's top two cocoa producers (with Côte d'Ivoire). Most chocolate you eat at home probably has cocoa from this part of West Africa in it.

foods.name: Jollof rice (Ghana style)
foods.description: The Ghanaian jollof: long-grain rice, tomato, pepper, chicken stock, smokier and less salty than the Nigerian version. Ghanaians will tell you it is the best in West Africa.

foods.name: Banku and tilapia
foods.description: Banku is a fermented corn dough cooked into a smooth ball, eaten with grilled tilapia from the lake or coast and a spicy "shito" pepper sauce. A Ghanaian classic.

foods.name: Fufu
foods.description: Pounded cassava and plantain mashed into a soft, springy dough, eaten with light tomato soup or peanut soup. Always eaten with your hands.

foods.name: Kelewele
foods.description: Cubed ripe plantain seasoned with ginger, chilli and other spices, then deep-fried until caramelised. The classic Accra street snack.

foods.name: Waakye
foods.description: Rice cooked with red beans (the beans tint the rice purple-brown), served with stew, gari (cassava grains), fried plantain, salad and chilli. A Ghanaian breakfast staple.

foods.name: Red red
foods.description: Black-eyed pea stew cooked with palm oil (giving it the deep red colour) and served with fried sweet plantain on the side. A vegetarian favourite.

scavengerItems.label: Kente cloth (bright woven royal cloth, every pattern means something)
Cape Coast or Elmina Castle (former colonial fort on the coast, now a memorial)
A Black Stars (Ghanaian national team) jersey or a street football game
Adinkra symbols stamped on cloth or painted on a wall
Cocoa pods growing on a tree (Ghana is a top-2 cocoa producer)
A drumming circle (or just a single drummer practising)
Accra rush-hour traffic with hawkers selling EVERYTHING out of car windows
Lake Volta, one of the largest man-made lakes in the world
Ashanti brass goldweights (intricate little figures once used to weigh gold dust)
Elephants in Mole National Park (the only park in West Africa where you can walk with them)
The Kakum National Park canopy walkway (rope bridges 40 m up in the rainforest)

geoMatches.description: The capital, on the Atlantic coast. A mix of beachside neighbourhoods (Labadi), market districts (Makola) and modern shopping streets. The Black Star Square is named after Ghana's independence.
A small Atlantic city with two huge colonial slave-trade castles (Cape Coast and Elmina), now powerful UNESCO-listed memorials.
The capital of the old Ashanti kingdom, the cultural heart of the Akan people. Home to the Manhyia Palace, where the Asantehene (king) still rules ceremonially.
A man-made lake created by damming the Volta River in the 1960s. It covers 8,500 square kilometres, one of the largest reservoirs on Earth.
Ghana's biggest national park, in the north. Home to elephants, antelopes, baboons and warthogs, and famous for letting you walk with the elephants.
A rainforest park with a famous suspension-bridge canopy walkway, 40 metres above the forest floor. Birds, butterflies and (rarely) forest elephants below.

senses.smell: Smoke from charcoal grills, sweet plantain frying in oil, the deep red palm-oil tang of "red red" stew, salt and seaweed on Labadi beach, fresh cocoa beans drying in the sun…
senses.hear: Highlife music and Afrobeats from every taxi and shop, "Akwaaba!" everywhere you arrive, the rhythm of talking drums, trotro mates shouting destinations out of doors, the constant hum of Accra traffic…
senses.taste: Spicy-sweet kelewele plantains, smooth banku scooped through fish stew, smoky Ghanaian jollof, ginger and pineapple sobolo (hibiscus juice), the bitter-sweet of pure Ghanaian dark chocolate…
senses.feel: Hot West African sun, salty Atlantic breeze in Accra, the bouncy spring of fufu in your fingers, soft warm cocoa pods, the bumpy bench of a packed trotro…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are Kente patterns special?
stories.body: Kente is a brightly coloured handwoven cloth from the Akan people of Ghana. Each cloth is made by weaving narrow strips on a wooden loom (each strip about 10 cm wide), then sewing the strips together to make a big rectangle. Every pattern has a name and a meaning: "Sika Futuro" (gold dust) is about wealth, "Adwen Asa" (wisdom is finished) is about knowledge, "Emaa Da" (it has not happened before) is about new beginnings. Originally kente was only worn by the Ashanti king and his court, but today Ghanaians wear it for weddings, graduations and special celebrations. African graduation gowns at universities across the world often feature a kente stole.

stories.question: How much of the world's chocolate comes from Ghana?
stories.body: About 60% of all the cocoa beans on Earth come from just two neighbouring West African countries: Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana. Cocoa was first brought to Ghana in 1879 by a blacksmith called Tetteh Quarshie, who smuggled some cocoa pods home from a trip to Equatorial Guinea. He planted them in his garden in Mampong. Within 30 years cocoa was Ghana's biggest export. Yet for most of the 1900s, most Ghanaian cocoa farmers never actually tasted chocolate, because the beans were shipped raw to factories in Europe and the USA to be made into the finished bars. That is slowly changing now, with more Ghanaian chocolate companies making bars at home.

stories.question: Why is Cape Coast a hard but important place to visit?
stories.body: Between the 1500s and the 1800s, around 12 million Africans were captured and shipped across the Atlantic to be enslaved in the Americas, in the largest forced human movement in history. Cape Coast Castle and Elmina Castle (a few kilometres up the coast) were two of the biggest "departure points". The captives were held in dark dungeons under the castles, sometimes for months, before being walked through a small door, the "Door of No Return", straight onto ships. Today both castles are UNESCO World Heritage memorials. Visitors of all backgrounds come to walk through the dungeons in silence. In 2019, Ghana invited the African diaspora around the world to "come home" for the 400th anniversary of the first ships, the famous "Year of Return".
stories.thinkingQuestion: A "Year of Return" invited descendants of enslaved Africans to come home and reconnect. Are there places that feel like "home" for your family even if you weren't born there?

stories.question: Who was Kwame Nkrumah?
stories.body: On 6 March 1957, the British flag came down at midnight in Accra and the new Ghanaian flag (red, gold, green, with a Black Star) went up. Ghana had become the FIRST country in sub-Saharan Africa to win independence from European colonial rule. The man who led it, Kwame Nkrumah, became Ghana's first president. He believed Africa should be united into one continent-wide federation (the "Pan-African" idea), and built schools, roads and hospitals at a huge pace. The Volta Dam (which made Lake Volta) was one of his biggest projects. He was overthrown in a coup in 1966 and died in exile, but he is still one of the most respected figures in African history.

stories.question: What is an Adinkra symbol?
stories.body: Adinkra are visual symbols invented by the Akan people of Ghana. There are around 80 traditional symbols, and each one stands for an idea, a proverb, or a piece of wisdom. "Gye Nyame" (a swirling shape) means "except for God" and is one of the most common. "Sankofa" (a bird with its head turned back) means "go back and fetch it", as in: look at the past to understand the future. "Akoma" (a heart shape) means patience and tolerance. The symbols are stamped onto special black-and-white funeral cloth, woven into kente, carved into stools, painted on walls, and now used everywhere from book covers to phone cases. Each one is a tiny lesson packed into a shape.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Ghana so far?
Have you tried saying "akwaaba" or "medaase" to anyone? How did they react?
Coast, forest, market, city, which has felt the most "Ghana"?
If you had your own kente pattern, what would it mean?
Ghana grows the cocoa that makes our chocolate. Does that change how you think about chocolate?
Ghana was the first sub-Saharan country to win independence. Why does going first matter?

wordSearch.hint: Ghanaian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: West African dwarf goat
animals.description: Everywhere in every village, on the road, eating roadside weeds.

animals.name: Mona monkey
animals.description: In the sacred Boabeng-Fiema Monkey Sanctuary, where the monkeys are protected by local belief.

animals.name: Tropical butterflies
animals.description: Kakum National Park is famous for hundreds of butterfly species, biggest as a small bird.

animals.name: Crocodile
animals.description: At Paga in the far north, you can sit next to friendly sacred crocs in the village pond.

animals.name: African elephant
animals.description: In Mole National Park you can walk with the elephants on foot, the only park in West Africa that lets you.

animals.name: Kob antelope
animals.description: A pale honey-coloured antelope common in Mole, often in herds.

animals.name: Bushbuck
animals.description: Shy reddish-brown forest antelope, especially in Kakum.
